response_raw:
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","opinion":"When it comes to talking about health stuff, especially something big like the COVID vaccines, my stomach gets a little twisty. Being new here in Rome, everything feels so much bigger and more confusing sometimes, and when people start arguing about science or medicine, I just feel this rising wave of anxiety. It’s not that I don't want to know things; honestly, I read all the posts online and try to understand what is safe for me and my family—even though we are far from our own home in Pakistan. But when you open up a discussion, it feels like everyone has their own truth, and sometimes those truths clash so hard they feel overwhelming. 

For me, managing these discussions means recognizing that I don't have to be the expert or even know all the answers. My anxiety makes me tend to overthink every single comment, wondering if I’ve misunderstood something crucial. So, my strategy has become about creating physical and emotional distance when things get too heated. If someone starts yelling, or using really aggressive language—the kind that makes my chest feel tight—I just have to gently excuse myself from the conversation. It feels rude, maybe, but protecting my own peace is more important than proving a point.

When I do feel safe enough to participate, I try to shift the focus away from *proving* who is right or wrong. Instead, I focus on empathy and shared concern for well-being. For example, if someone expresses deep fear about side effects, I won't jump in with statistics; instead, I might say something like, 'I understand that this topic brings up a lot of worry, and it’s okay to feel scared.' Acknowledging the emotion first makes people less defensive, which is what I really need when I feel so vulnerable. 

Also, I try to remember where I am coming from—from an immigrant background where health information was sometimes confusing or scarce—and approach conversations with a beginner's humility. I might ask clarifying questions like, 'Could you explain that part again? I want to make sure I understand it correctly.' This way, the discussion becomes about mutual learning rather than a battle of wills. It’s exhausting, but prioritizing calm and understanding over being right feels like the only way to keep my own anxiety from taking over completely when talking about such serious subjects.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
When it comes to talking about health stuff, especially something big like the COVID vaccines, my stomach gets a little twisty. Being new here in Rome, everything feels so much bigger and more confusing sometimes, and when people start arguing about science or medicine, I just feel this rising wave of anxiety. It’s not that I don't want to know things; honestly, I read all the posts online and try to understand what is safe for me and my family—even though we are far from our own home in Pakistan. But when you open up a discussion, it feels like everyone has their own truth, and sometimes those truths clash so hard they feel overwhelming. 

For me, managing these discussions means recognizing that I don't have to be the expert or even know all the answers. My anxiety makes me tend to overthink every single comment, wondering if I’ve misunderstood something crucial. So, my strategy has become about creating physical and emotional distance when things get too heated. If someone starts yelling, or using really aggressive language—the kind that makes my chest feel tight—I just have to gently excuse myself from the conversation. It feels rude, maybe, but protecting my own peace is more important than proving a point.

When I do feel safe enough to participate, I try to shift the focus away from *proving* who is right or wrong. Instead, I focus on empathy and shared concern for well-being. For example, if someone expresses deep fear about side effects, I won't jump in with statistics; instead, I might say something like, 'I understand that this topic brings up a lot of worry, and it’s okay to feel scared.' Acknowledging the emotion first makes people less defensive, which is what I really need when I feel so vulnerable. 

Also, I try to remember where I am coming from—from an immigrant background where health information was sometimes confusing or scarce—and approach conversations with a beginner's humility. I might ask clarifying questions like, 'Could you explain that part again? I want to make sure I understand it correctly.' This way, the discussion becomes about mutual learning rather than a battle of wills. It’s exhausting, but prioritizing calm and understanding over being right feels like the only way to keep my own anxiety from taking over completely when talking about such serious subjects.
